article_title: NASA's Van Allen Probe A Re-Enters Atmosphere Eight Years Ahead of Schedule as Solar Maximum Accelerates Orbital Decay

content_preview.title: NASA's Van Allen Probe A Re-Enters Atmosphere Eight Years Ahead of Schedule as Solar Maximum Accelerates Orbital Decay
content_preview.summary: The 1,300-pound radiation belt spacecraft burned up over the eastern Pacific Ocean on March 11, ending a 14-year journey that began with a two-year mission and yielded the discovery of a transient third radiation belt around Earth.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA's Van Allen Probe A re-entered Earth's atmosphere at 6:37 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, March 11, [the agency confirmed](https://www.nasa.gov/missions/van-allen-probes/nasa-van-allen-probe-a-to-re-enter-atmosphere/), ending almost 14 years in orbit for a spacecraft originally designed to last two. The U.S. Space Force verified that the probe came down over the [eastern Pacific Ocean at approximately 2 degrees south latitude and 255.3 degrees east longitude](https://www.nasa.gov/missio...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article at 491 words. Clear section organization covering mission background, scientific legacy, early re-entry explanation, risk assessment, and future outlook. Appropriate technical depth without jargon overload.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 sources fetched and verified. NASA.gov confirms re-entry time (6:37 AM EDT, March 11), coordinates (2°S, 255.3°E), launch date, mission duration, solar maximum acceleration, and Probe B timeline. CBS News confirms Johns Hopkins APL operation, two-year primary mission, third radiation belt discovery, hundreds of publications, and 1-in-4,200 harm probability. NBC News independently confirms the 1-in-4,200 risk figure and solar activity impact. Space.com confirms the 1,300-pound weight and re-entry details.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims map correctly to cited sources. Minor note: NBC gives weight as 1,323 lbs while article uses Space.com's rounded 1,300 lbs — acceptable rounding. The 'almost 14 years' claim is accurate (Aug 2012 to March 2026 = ~13.5 years). Atlas V rocket is correct per public record.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism despite the dramatic subject matter. Phrases like 'a timeline compression driven by' and 'those projections were overtaken by reality' are factual, not editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: No prior articles on Van Allen Probes in the archive. Fresh topic covering a March 11 event.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality submission with strong source attribution, accurate facts, and clean neutral tone. Ready for publication.
